summary: The previous RTL used both posedge and negedge clock assignments to q, causing multi-driven warnings and likely simulation mismatches. The q logic was convoluted and did not match the expected set/reset flip-flop behavior inferred from the waveform. This patch replaces the q and p logic with a single always @(posedge clock) block, implementing a state machine where q acts as a set/reset flip-flop: q is set to 1 when a==1, and reset to 0 when a==0. p is asserted (1) on the clock edge when a==1, otherwise 0. This matches the observed waveform transitions and eliminates multi-driven signals.
risks: If the waveform requires q to remain high across multiple cycles after a==1, this implementation will only keep q high for one cycle unless a remains high.
If the reference design uses a more complex state machine for q, further adjustment may be needed.
Initial values may still cause a mismatch if the reference uses a different initialization.
next_steps: Run simulation and compare output waveforms for p and q against the reference.
If mismatches persist, analyze the exact timing and persistence of q after a==1.
If needed, adjust q logic to implement a true set/reset flip-flop (set on a==1, reset on a==0, but only reset when a==0 and q==1).
